author: @atrumluminarium
text: The reason why plain plastic worked the same as the lens is because the solar collection area remained the same with both options. The only difference is that a plane transmits the radiation evenly while a lens points the same amount of energy all towards the focus. A lens would give you an advantage if it was larger than your collection window and focusing the light to all go through said window.

author: @Pwecko
text:
Something very strange is happening. I woke up yesterday with an idea for a way to distil clean water from seawater. I made a couple of drawings and explained in writing how it would work. Then, today, this video came up in my feed. This has happened a few times. Is YouTube reading my mind? And no, I didn't search for anything about solar stills that might prompt YouTube to recommend this video. 
Anyway, my idea was for something much bigger that would serve a community. The foam and carbon material would enhance my design, I'm sure. Thank you.

author: @fajile5109
text:
Here my idea. Take two copper plates and polish them to a mirror then use cvd to cover one surface with carbon rods drill a hole in the one with the carbon coating then focus a fresnel lens down into the drill hole also space the two copper plates 5mm apart. The light from the fresnel should bounce around the copper plates until the top one absorbs the energy this should get insanely hot like itll boil water im sure. Maybe even melt the copper.
Oh i mean to use this copper plates as a heat for cooking. Basically the copper plates can be sized to the lens.
The reason your lens isnt working that great is simple if you have 4 photons and you condense them into a beam you still only have 4 photons. The fresnel lens works better when its heating a tiny point when a larger lens. If you cut the size of the evaporation pool by 4x it would boil that water very quickly.

author: @williambianchi2006
text:
Will a solar oven or solar cooker get hot enough to boil a kettle of water? If it will, couldn't a copper tube attached to the kettle and run out of the side of the oven, or cooker, which is then coiled and guided to drain into a collection container basically distill that water. Seems a passive system after putting the kettle of water in and attaching the copper tube. Walk away and let the sun do it's thing.
Line inside of cooker with a reflective film or actual mirrors, put glass over the top, and paint the kettle black.
Same setup as a fire fueled distiller, but using the sun and a solar oven as the heat source.
Lots could be wrong with this method. Maybe the box will not get hot enough or maybe a kettle would take too long to get hot. It's just a thought.

author: @mrchin7562
text:
The amount of solar energy you let into the box will determine how much water evaporates. That's energy conservation. If you want to improve the rate of evaporation, supply more energy per square foot, or more square feet. Since the transparent sheet is sloped, you'll need to face it so the maximum area is sunlit. Works better in shallow water, naturally, because the water will heat up faster. You've already increased surface area by using the foam. To supply more energy per square foot, (AND increase surface area further - due to leaf structure) try adding plants. Moss, perhaps. They'll draw up water in the roots and the water they don't use will evaporate and condense on the plastic. Plants work at night, too, still drawing water up and whatever sugar they consume in respiration will release water vapour. Increasing the angle of slope will decrease the size of water droplets before they start to slide down towards the collector. For saltwater, use saltwater plants.

The video *is* interesting, but there are other important factors, not just the ones in the paper.

author: @Primer595
text: The area of the fresnel  lense is  immaterial. The sun is shining on a fixed area and the lense can only concentrate the same amount of sunlight onto a smaller more localised area. The net amout of heat is the same. That is why a plain lense ie plastic sheet was just as good as the fresnel which was doing the same job. If you could concentrate the solar energy from numerous fresnel lenses onto the area you are using then that would help your experimental outcome. Nice job.

author: @3dmaker699
text: Robert, I live in Australia and we have very intense droughts but a heap of space and lots of direct sunlight.  I've had this idea for 30 -40 years now .  I would like to do a solar power station in the outback for the sole task of pumping seawater from the coast in thick water pipes .  But the water pipes are built with spiral carbon lining so over a great distance as the water spirals through the pipes over distance by the time the water reaches its destination you have fresh water and at  points along the way you have huge underground storage tanks. The system at first would be expensive to set up but over time it would more than pay as water makes crops and food and whole farming towns would spring up.  Train tacks are expensive but we have services through out Australia. Why not  solar water systems ?

author: @malakilab
text:
Some kind of hydrophobic transparent coating would do it, but i am concerned if it will condensate even at all in first place. The lens to try focusing even more was a good idea tho.Having the sun entry more on the side and using reflectors would also fix the problem, as light would enter under plate collector plate. You would have the advantage not needing it to be opaque, making it with aluminium and have a heatsink in the water, both to cool the plate and improve condensation, but further warm the water. It's a problem also i think with the glass, as i think it would get significantly hotter if exposed to a lot of sunlight. reducing efficiency. 
Thank you for those two videos, i wanted to build something similar for a long time to distillate rain water tank. Gave me so many ideas on how to improve the system. The foam can be some kind of barrier too for external contaminants, letting rain water in when it rains, adding another layer of "filtration". A back washing mechanism would be awesome if you want it somewhat self rejuvenating.
Good luck with other projects!

author: @bazofiascbr
text:
Just remember, distilled water is _not_ that adequate for drinking, it lacks salts essential for proper human hydration. But if you have distilled water you can always add them! Chemists provide inexpensive hydrating powders (for severe dehydration cases) that I suppose could overcome this issue.
But in distilled water is often much much better than contaminated or salted sea water! Good job!

author: @PressToDigitate
text: ROBERT:  Isn't that the same Carbon Felt you used in the Evaporative Charge Separation experiment?  Since the two (Thermal Evaporation & Charge Separation) are interrelated - and they are, so much so we've talked about configurations of exploiting it for Charge - *All You Have To Do* is work out the best way to think that in reverse, inducing Charge Separation (or Neutralization) so as to maximize the Evaporation Process.  There are published papers on the electrodynamic aspects of the gas/liquid boundary layer.   What IF a very minor change to the electrostatics might substantially accelerate evaporation?   A small Photovoltaic (or Thermoelectric) Cell attached to the Solar Still could have outsized effects, far beyond the Caloric energy value of the electric current used to alter the boundary layer electrodynamics; likely a catalytic effect.

author: @Therapistinthewhitehouse
text: I’m puzzled as to why you thought a lens would make any difference whatsoever? Lenses don’t create energy they focus it, therefore they both receive the same amount of energy. For every given area there is a specific amount of solar energy hitting it, if the lens is the same size as a still with a plain piece of glass the only difference will be that the lens will focus the majority of the energy in one spot and have cooler areas elsewhere, whereas the one with plain sheet of glass has an even temperature all over. Or am I missing something?

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: I like your extension of the survival water 'still.  I think that your implementation suffers from the same limitation in that your evaporation and condensation mechanisms are the same, and that your collection and supply are on the same end of the container.  Using a condensing stack on the high side, and making an allowance for air convection from low-to-high, might greatly improve efficiency.  Air convection from evaporator to condenser might also keep your lens clean, making the fresnel lens more useful.  Your reservoir could also be made more cheaply using waste cellulose fiber instead of styrene foam.

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
That condensation issue was a thing I've been pondering on since I saw your last video, and was trying to figure out if a lens would be worthwhile, or if there was a way to angle it so the lensed light could hit the carbon felt, without having to go through a foggy medium- could you bounce the light in under the collector sheet with a couple mirrors?  If you could do That, then you could go with an opaque, more contained condenser. (I had some concern about seawater contamination from waves splashing up inside of it and splashing your collector- not a problem in a still source, but it crossed my mind.)  
Awesome work as always :)

replies.author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
replies.text: I also had a thought, if you had some consistent wind, if you could make a venturi or a little solar fan on the top end to suck up the humid air and run that through a hose to a jug underwater, where it being cooler would help with the condensation and speed up production?  It seems that the airflow might help keep the lens, plastic, or whatever clearer and should improve performance in all cases wouldn't you think?

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
Glad you noticed that a lens made no difference. ~( 'w')/

Glass is definitely most highly recommended for several reasons.

Make some outriggers like a catamaran out of some PVC pipe and have your discharge pipe out the front and let it drop into its own PVC pipe with a little bit of filter to keep out bugs and you should be good to go on less than choppy seas.

If there is fear of it drifting a way, simply apply a tether to it so it remains attached to something you will be able to grab hold of it by [as in if you were out to sea, tether it to your boat, but on land (such as a pond, puddle, pool, or lagoon, for instance) maybe use a stick].

author: @bigunone
text: Back in the 80s either Popular Mechanics, or Mother Earth News, built one of these to be set on your roof with a tube running down to a Igloo cooler on the counter. They used just regular glass, washed black aquarium gravel in the bottom, a float valve to control the water and a copper gutter to catch the water and send it down to the catch can

replies.author: @dustinsmith8341
replies.text: And then it was made illegal in some states? lol

replies.author: @bigunone
replies.text: @dustinsmith8341 Not as far as I know I have done an internet searches , But haven't found it, I can see it in my mind, but can't remember which magazine I used to read it was in. There have been several articles like that

author: @pulesjet
text: Thinking you could cool the condenser section some how ?  Small Solar Cells and Peltier unit cooling a collection plate ?  Don't need much cooling but some would improve results.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: I was thinking of maybe suspending some type of condensing "grid" between the glass and the felt, that would allow most of the sunlight to pass through but upon which the steam would condense... maybe the grid could be attached to a peltier unit to cool the grid by conduction?  As you said, it wouldn't need much cooling, just enough to cause condensation.  The grid could be angled sharply so that the water runs down it into collection tubes.

text: Impressive as always, good work!

text: You need to put the lens smooth side in. Problem solved,

text: Haha! I was just watching a bunch of videos on things to do with pool noodles this morning. They'd make a great float for this :)

author: @lubbock2704
text: The reason the fresnel lens did not work noticeably better than a plain sheet of aplastic is likely due to the water droplets changing the refractivity of the lense as they form, this would be especially true if the fresnel lens's ridges were pointed inwards towards the carbon felt.

replies.author: @fritanke2318
replies.text: Just adding my comment for the AI ranking. That said I was wondering if the lens had any purpose as it would just focus the energy at 1 pont in the felt and not add total energy inflow in that design.

replies.author: @lubbock2704
replies.text: @fritanke2318 I agree that focusing the light on a single point would not be helpful overall, however if you have a linier lense it can help to accumulate light from different angles that would otherwise not have hit the carbon felt.

author: @guypehaim1080
text: Maybe if you use E-glass to contain the heat, the evaporation rate would be higher.  Also, you could make the base higher to raise the pipe so a container could be put under the end of it.

author: @ServantScientistSonSaved
text: Keep going Robert. Down in Antigua and Barbuda, we're in a drought. I need a way to water my garden with sea water.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: @SeeTheWholeTruth Key line plowing and swale land preparation look into that if you want to keep your soil and improve the land in general.

replies.author: @johnmcfadden9336
replies.text: There is a Dutch man experimenting with 50/50 sea/ fresh water for irrigation and on some crops works very well  the increase in salt causes the plants to produce more sugar to counter balance the osmotic effect of the salt  sweeter tomatoes 😋

replies.author: @garethb5729
replies.text:
I always imagined the solution to this requiring a tube in a tube. 

The internal tube being a semi permeable hydrofilic material. 
Running the salt water in the internal tube and collect the fresh water from the external.

replies.author: @fallknight5405
replies.text: Celery can be watered with sea water if you don't mind a garden full of celery

replies.author: @ronaldd2154
replies.text: Can you not dig a well?

author: @btryba
text:
Low-e coated glass (modern window glass) might work. But then again it might trap too much heat and it might not condense. 

If only there was a way to cool the glass. Since it is all sealed. Perhaps putting some foam on the glass in the "shade section" so that it is in the shade and then use contaminated water on the foam to cause evaporation and cool the surface of the glass.
